notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s a framework you can use on your own books to judge whether your business credit card balances are risky and whether it’s safe to chase rewards.

1) Quick risk check (5 factors)

For each card (and overall), rate these 1–3
1 = low risk, 3 = high risk. Then see where you land most often.

A. Utilization % (per card and total)
Formula: Balance ÷ Credit limit × 100
Interpretation:
Low: under 30% on each card and overall
Medium: 30–70%
High: over 70% or maxed out
B. Ability to pay in full

Look at the last 3–6 statements:

Low risk: Paid statement balance in full every month
Medium: Occasionally carry a balance, but could pay in full from cash if needed
High: Regularly cannot pay in full; only paying minimums or small amounts above
C. Interest cost

Add up interest charges over the last 3 months:

Low: $0 (always pay in full)
Medium: Some interest, but <2–3% of monthly revenue
High: Interest is large or growing, or >3–5% of monthly revenue
D. Payment history

Review last 12 months:

Low: 0 late payments; all on time
Medium: 1 late (caught up quickly, no pattern)
High: 2+ lates, any 30/60/90‑day delinquencies, or using one card to pay another
E. Cash reserves vs balances

Compare cash + savings to total card balances:

Low: Cash ≥ 3× average monthly card spend, and ≥ total card balances
Medium: Cash covers 1–3 months of card spend, but < total balances
High: Cash < 1 month of card spend, or you’d struggle to pay off cards within 3–6 months

If you’re mostly in the “high” column, your balances are generally risky. Mostly “low” is usually manageable.

2) Concrete thresholds for small‑business risk

Use these as general ranges, not hard rules:

How to read this:

Low risk on 4–6 rows → Chasing rewards is usually safer if you keep paying in full.
Medium risk on several rows → Be cautious; focus on stability first.
High risk on 2+ rows → Rewards should generally be secondary to reducing balances and risk.
3) Checklist before chasing rewards or bonuses

Run through this before opening or leaning on a card for rewards:

Can I pay in full every month?

Check the last 6 statements: were they all paid in full?
If not, treat rewards as a nice‑to‑have later, not a priority now.

Will this bonus push me to overspend?

Look at the sign‑up bonus requirement vs normal spend:
If the bonus needs $X in 3 months, compare that to your average 3‑month spend in those categories.
If you’d have to increase spending just to hit the bonus, that’s a red flag.

Impact on cash flow

Map out the next 3 months:
Expected revenue
Fixed expenses (payroll, rent, software, taxes)
Planned card spend
Make sure you can:
Cover all fixed costs
Pay the card in full
Still keep your minimum cash buffer (e.g., 1–3 months of expenses)

Credit score and utilization

Check current utilization on all cards.
Try to avoid:
Pushing any card above ~50–70%
Applying for multiple new cards in a short period if you’ll need financing soon (loans, lines of credit)

Card terms and fees

Confirm:
APR (in case you do carry a balance)
Annual fee and when it posts
Foreign transaction fees (if relevant)
Penalty APR for late payments
Ask: “Does the expected value of rewards after year 1 exceed the annual fee, based on my normal spend?”

Rewards structure fit

Match card bonus categories to your actual business spending:
If most spend is on software/ads but the card rewards dining/travel, the fit is weak.
Prioritize cards where your existing, necessary spend earns most of the rewards.

Exit plan if revenue dips

Decide ahead of time:
When you stop using the card (e.g., if utilization >50% or cash <1 month of expenses)
How you’ll pay down any balance quickly if a big client pays late
4) Using Monarch to separate business vs personal

Even without a formal business entity, you can keep things clean in Monarch:

Tags for business vs personal

Create tags like:
Business-Expense
Business-Revenue
Business-Card-1, Business-Card-2
Tag:
All transactions on business‑use cards
Any business expenses on personal cards (until you fully separate)

Notes on transactions

For ambiguous charges (Amazon, Costco, etc.), add a note like:
“Inventory for Q2 project”
“Client lunch – marketing”
This makes it easier later to see what was necessary vs bonus‑driven.

Custom categories or subcategories

Create business‑focused categories:
“Biz: Software”
“Biz: Advertising”
“Biz: Travel”
That helps you see what’s business‑critical vs more discretionary.

Saved filters / reports

Filter by:
Tag = Business-Expense
Account = your business‑use cards
Then:
Review or export monthly totals
Calculate utilization and interest cost from statements

Future: business entity setup

Once you can configure a business entity, you could:
Move business accounts into that entity
Restrict business reports to those accounts
Track business cash flow, utilization, and interest separately from personal

If you want to go deeper, you can share (outside this environment) a few example numbers like utilization, months carrying a balance, and cash vs balances, and th
